text: This is our third video in the series of the use of ICG in surgery.
We uploaded two previous videos, one concerned with biliary surgery, use of ICG to demonstrate the biliary anatomy, accessory ducts, compound ducts and leaks of bile through the gallbladder belt.
Our second video was on the demonstration of the vascularity of different bowel segments after intravenous injection of ICG.
Our third video, this one, is concerned with the potential for lymphatic mapping of ICG and using of fluorescence guided lymphadenectomy.
This is a case of CA cardia.
The patient has received neoadjuvant chemotherapy and now we are going to inject ICG in the cardiac area to perform the lymphatic mapping.
We first inject normal saline and then we proceed to injection of the ICG after creating swelling.
We are now injecting normal saline in the area of the tumor to create an elevation swelling in which we are going to inject ICG so no spillage of ICG occurs.
Now we are injecting ICG.
The ICG is the green oily substance that is going to come out of the cannula now.
And we are injecting it sub-depository in the previous elevated area by normal saving.
And we should repeat this injection in different parts of the lesion.
